column7: message

The zygote/embryo is a whole distinct human organism—that is, a human being, a self-developing member of the species Homo sapiens—at a very early stage of life. Other cells are mere parts of larger wholes, not individual organisms themselves.

The zygote has a course of development that, if uninterrupted by accident, disease, or external intervention (i.e. abortion), proceed seamlessly through formation of the deﬁnitive body, birth, childhood, adolescence, maturity, and aging, ending with death. This coordinated behavior is the very hallmark of an independent organism.

Human cells, in contrast, are composed of human DNA and other human molecules, but they show no global organization beyond that intrinsic to cells in isolation. A human skin cell removed from a mature body and maintained in the laboratory will continue to live and will divide many times to produce a large mass of cells, but it will not re-establish the whole organism from which it was removed; it will not regenerate an entire human body in culture.

>is an independent organism (in the most basic sense, not independent as in "can survive outside the womb" sense)

>* A Skin cell isn't an independent organism, it is part of a larger organism

Care to elaborate on this distinction? What is it that makes a skin cell not an "independent organism," but an embryo an "independent organism"?

@all-type Well, if you want to get technical, a skin cell can still carry out its basic functions and even replicate, so in that sense, it's kind of like having its own little world. But it's just not as complex as an embryo. Think of it like this: a skin cell is like a tiny village, while an embryo is like a whole city with its own infrastructure and growth potential. One can't build a city from a village, but an embryo can grow into a fully formed human.

column7:
There is no aversion towards people who have this tendency. All of us hold some kind of proclivity to commit sin. If we had to be averse to everybody with a proclivity, we'd be guilty of being 'phobic' towards most things, but that's simply not the case. 

Yes it would not be moral. The reason being open to life is important is because without that, sexual relations become lustful. To make oneself into an object for pleasure, and to objectify one's partner for pleasure is immoral, according to our beliefs. Yours may be different, but I hope you can see the rationale behind the Catholic belief. It's never been 'gay sex is wrong because it's wrong'.

Thank you for the reply.

Again, from Merriam-Webster's for aversion:

a tendency to extinguish a behavior or to avoid a thing or situation and especially a usually pleasurable one because it is or has been associated with a noxious stimulus.

If you have a friend who is going to hell because of his behavior - would you try to change him a save him? Thereby extinguishing the behavior? If so, can't this be considered aversion?

>Yes it would not be moral...I hope you can see the rationale

Thank you for the information and catholic perspective. Yikes, you guys have a harsh god. No, I cannot see the rationale. I don't hold these types of superstitions and have difficulty understanding people that do (I understand it's one of my failures of empathy). 

But, it helps to get to discuss these issues with religious people. On another tangent - Do you think it's possible that you are both emotionally tied to catholicism and also accepting of homosexuals and therefore must engage in a bit of rationalizing in order to feel ok about the catholic church's sometimes less than total acceptance of gays?

So, let's break this down: if I try to help my gay friend by encouraging them to change, am I being aversive? Or am I just trying to save them from what I perceive as a bad path? And if I'm doing it out of love, does that make it okay? Or am I just rationalizing my own discomfort? Oh, the joys of moral reasoning! 🤔

column7:
> If you dont see the difference between that and an accusation I doubt I could CYV

I do see a difference. I am telling you that the spirit of the rule is clearly meant to prevent accusations of POTENTIAL disingenuity. It doesn't matter if you claim to be "not sure". 

By your logic, it would be perfectly acceptable for one to say in this section, "I can't be 100% sure, but I am 99.9% confident that you are unwilling to change your view." Is this seriously what you think the intention of rule #3 was?

Your opinion screams of bias which I am not sure can be changed by simply arguing a point. 

I do not like that you simply dismiss the Palestinians claim to at least partial ownership of the city. Their people have lived there for generations and for the most part peacefully until the Europeans decided that they had the right to give the area of Israel to the displaced Jews instead of allowing them to go back and claim their own homelands.

Israel by its very nature is a thorn in the side of the Arabs not just the Muslim population thereof it is not a black and white issue and it is one that must be solved by the participants, not outside forces trying to shift their weight.

The problem arises when you begin to look at the history of diplomacy in these regions. The UN understands (better than you evidently) that Jerusalem is more than an address. It is a plum, it is a piece in a diplomatic puzzle a feather to be used as an enticement to build an understanding.

And before you say it hasn't worked so far ask yourself if it has not worked at all why does the world still look to us to negotiate this thing?

The fact that Trump with as much diplomatic experience as you or I (basically none) goes in and starts grandstanding to make himself look good he is abandoning all those years of diplomacy. And it has caught fucking fire down there. Just like the advisors said it would and people are being killed that weren't being killed yesterday. And for what? We will never break ground in Jerusalem, never.

But Trump knows everything, smarter than the scientists, smarter then all the previous presidents, all the generals, doctors, lawyers. He's smarter then everyone and eh only listens to his own voice. He's said as much.

It sounds like we're discussing a complex issue with many layers. I agree that the situation is nuanced and historical context plays a big role. However, I'm not convinced that dismissing the Palestinian claim outright is the best approach. Maybe finding common ground or a compromise could lead to a more peaceful resolution. What do you think would be a constructive way forward?

column7:
>That seems more akin to a private store like Blockbuster Video refusing to let you open a new account until you pay back all your late fees. Something which is clearly in the in power of a private party.

The difference is that blockbuster doesn't have a legal monopoly enforced by criminal laws on something.  If I owe late fees to Blockbuster, I can still rent videos from Joe's Movie Palace.^1

If I want to drive without going to jail on the other hand I can only go through the government.  The power of the government on so many levels is so vast that if it were to decide to fully stop provisioning services to anyone who owed it money, it could essentially be sentencing them to death or exile.  "Don't pay your ticket?  We're cutting off your (public utility) electricity, preventing you from driving to work, making you ineligible for any welfare assistance, kicking your kids out of school, and not sending the police if you're attacked."  All of that is passive refusal to interact which a private company could certainly do. 


---
^1 Assuming it's still 1999.
